pull_requests.title: Decouple AssImp from Scene API
pull_requests.body:
## What does this PR do?

We would like to use a different library for scene asset import yet still benefit from all offerings of the Scene API pipeline.
This PR reviews the interface between Scene API and AssImp and fixes the abstraction where it was leaking or non-existent.
It is an enabler for addition of different scene import libraries as Gems. By itself no functional changes were intended: all existing assets should be imported as before.

It removes references to AssImp specific code from the generic part of the SceneSystem/SceneImporter.
It enhances SceneWrapperBase with additional methods that provide clear boundaries.
It refactors AssImp SDKWrapper and AssimpImportContext to be provided by AssimpImportContextProvider factory.
It uses Abstract Factory Pattern to decouple AssImp specific ImportContext from SceneImporter
It implements an ImportContextRegistry Interface for registration of additional ImportContextProviders


## How was this PR tested?

I mostly tested inside internal project - but was using stabilization as a base. Therefore, recent additions (namely: #18686 #18800, #18829 and #18844) should be reviewed carefully.

pull_requests.title: Introduce Alternate Frame Rendering capability to the engine
pull_requests.body:
## What does this PR do?

This PR introduces alternate frame rendering (AFR) functionality to the engine by latching on to a `RenderPipeline`, capturing all passes that are not already scheduled to a fixed device (as well as the `CopyToSwapchain` pass which always runs on the default display device), alternating them between the devices over successive frames and inserting a `CopyPass` from the auxiliary GPUs to the display GPUs at the end, right before `CopyToSwapchain`.

AFR is currently enabled by adding a command lind parameter `--afr <PartialPipelineName>` (e.g. `--afr MainPipeline` to run for the MainPipeline (not an exact match as additional runtime parameters might be added to a pipeline name, like `ViewportId`), if no name is pipeline name is passed, it latches onto the first pipeline which has a `CopyToSwapChain` pass in it), and then applies AFR to this `RenderPipeline` every frame.
The `RenderPipeline` passed to AFR must have a `CopyToSwapChain` pass, otherwise an `AZ_Error` is raised and execution continues without AFR.

Although functional, the application of AFR is somewhat limited at the moment as custom interconnects have unfortunately been abandoned on most consumer cards currently on the market. 
That means that currently copies have to go through PCIe, which is significantly slower than custom interconnects (like SLI/Crossfire in the past or NVLink on some NVIDIA GPUs).
Furthermore, AFR only improves performance once CPU frame time is significantly less than the GPU frame time, as otherwise the CPU is not fast enough in submitting new frames so that the GPUs can even run in parallel.
That is why enabling AFR on a very simple level (like the Shaderball test scene) that is CPU bound will slow down the application, as we gain nothing from parallel submission (as the CPU is slower than the GPU), but also introduce additional overhead through the copy.
On the other hand, if a Pipeline + level has a higher shader load and is dominated by GPU frame time, AFR can improve performance (for the included scene, AFR improves the performance on two 4090s from around $10 ms$ to around $6.2ms$ (`dx12` on Windows or `vulkan` on Linux).

### Performance
Performance measurements for the included Shaderball level with many shaderballs to increase GPU load without impacting CPU load.
#### dx12 (without AFR)
![noAFR](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/53a33751-2b41-4b1e-af14-ed6da11a49a8)
#### dx12 (AFR)
![AFR](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e61c4a45-54d5-4606-a6b9-cfc3cf034c51)
#### vulkan (AFR) on Windows
`vulkan` performance on Windows is somewhat worse than `dx12`, as certain parts (like cross-device `Fence`s) are only supported on Linux, but still faster than without AFR.
![AFR_vulkan](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d525bccf-1582-4cf8-b4d9-2001e7aa169e)

## How was this PR tested?

Tested `Editor` and `GameLauncher` on both Windows (`dx12` and `vulkan`) and Linux (`vulkan`) with the standard Shaderball test level as well as with a custom [ShaderballLevelLarge.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/20899540/ShaderballLevelLarge.zip) (that places a large number of shaderballs instead of a single one) to show off the performance benefit on GPU-bound applications.

pull_requests.title: Add configuration files for Emscripten platform
pull_requests.body:
## What does this PR do?

Related to :
- https://github.com/o3de/o3de/issues/19083
- https://github.com/o3de/3p-package-source/pull/284

Allow O3DE to be configured by [Emscripten](https://emscripten.org/) compiler to target webassembly. Configuration will just fail on the missing 3p dependencies which will be progressively added by the PR above and its follow-ups.

Right now, removing occurences of these 3p-packages in the few cmake files using them will allow the configuration to succeed, and allow anyone to try building and start fixing compilation issues.

**Convention**

Emscripten is as much a platform as it is a compiler, they decided to use an [unix-like API](https://emscripten.org/docs/api_reference/Filesystem-API.html) for the file system etc but you could implement a C++ to WebAssembly compiler exposing windows-like API. This is why the Platform Abstraction Layer uses `emscripten` as a name instead of `wasm`

## How was this PR tested?

Tried on Windows development branch

Setup :

- Follow emscripten [install instruction](https://emscripten.org/docs/getting_started/downloads.html)
- Install ninja build system
- Build the 3p package from emscripten using the branch above
- In the emscripten o3de branch. Create a new folder "wasm", go inside, and run `emcmake cmake ..  -DLY_3RDPARTY_PATH=YOUR_PATH` -> As stated above, will currently fail here
- Then build with  `cmake --build .`
pull_requests.author: guillaume-haerinck
pull_requests.description: Allow O3DE to be configured by \[Emscripten\](https://emscripten.org/) compiler to target webassembly. Configuration will just fail on the missing 3p dependencies which will be progressively added by the PR above and its follow-ups.

pull_requests.title: Fixes blendshapes ("morph targets") not working
pull_requests.body: ## What does this PR do? Fixes https://github.com/o3de/o3de/issues/19058 It turns out that the blend shape data was getting the number of vertices from the mesh by dividing the position buffer by 3 (3 floats per vertex), but the position (and normal, and other) buffer was being aligned to some larger multiple of 16 bytes (by padding it), resulting in a larger buffer length than the actual number of vertices. Because of this issue, it was discarding otherwise good blend shapes, thinking that they were invalid. Instead, I get the number of vertices by using the m_numVertices value. ## How was this PR tested? Using the data provided in the above issue, I can now import the morph target data just fine, and use it in the animation editor in the animation editor: In blender

pull_requests.title: Fix Clang20 compile errors
pull_requests.body:
## What does this PR do?

This PR fixes compile errors for Clang20, which are mostly caused by more strict heuristics:
* Use `memcpy`/`memset` for non-trivially-copyable classes (either remove the memcpy call or make the class/struct trivially copyable)
  * In one instance (Task.cpp move constructor) this was not easily possible, so I just cast it to `void*` (which is also what the compiler suggests). Just copying or moving the members of the class did not work (test case failure), keeping the memcpy operation works for all testcases
* User-defined literals must not have a space between `operator ""` and the suffix

The change in the GradientSignal gem are weird: The OpenImageIO library uses some constexpr format strings which do not work with Clang20, but work normally in MSVC. I did not really find a solution other than disabling constexpr formt strings for the whole library.

## How was this PR tested?

Compile with Clang20 on Windows, AR

pull_requests.title: Remove AZ_FORCE_CPU_GPU_INSYNC macro from public Atom RHI header
pull_requests.body:
## What does this PR do?

This PR removes usages of the `AZ_FORCE_CPU_GPU_INSYNC` macro from public Atom-RHI headers and replaces it with the constexpr `AZ::RHI::ForceCpuGpuInSync` boolean variable, which is no longer used in RHI header files.

This has the advantage that only 16 targets need to be rebuilt (9 compile + 7 link targets), compared to 515 targets without this change (377 compile + 138 link targets; Editor target with AutomatedTesting project, unity build), when enabling/disabling insync, which is a huge time saver when debugging GPU bugs with a cold compiler cache.
Buffers with `FrameCountMax` versions now do not change their size with insync enabled, which might be inconvenient when trying to find a specific buffer by name in a GPU debugger (eg. Nsights Object Browser the "RayTracingMeshInfo" resource is there 3 times). It would be possible to work around this problem by using a dllexported function (to keep the rebuild target count small) which returns the actual framecount, and use this to allocate and cycle buffers in `RPI::RingBuffer` and related classes, but this would then not be a very elegant solution. Also, many objects making use of FrameCoundMax are scattered throughout the engine, so it would take many code changes to catch all of these.

(The diff looks way larger than it actually is, so whitespace-insensitive diff is preferrable.)

## How was this PR tested?

Run ASV and find a sample which crashes the GPU (eg. RHI/VariableRateShading with Rate4x4 selected), verify that the error message (last pass before crash) stays the same.
Run the Editor and verify that enabling insync reduces FPS below 60.

pull_requests.title: Fixes Many issues with Assetprocessor Dependencies
pull_requests.body:
I basically went through and looked for bad assets, errors in AP during startup, and anything related to the First Time User Experience (FTUE) of starting O3DE when you have no cache yet (so completely empty cache!)

Here are what I found, and this PR fixes.

## What does this PR do?

* Database bug was causing "Get Source by Job Id" to return empty results. This caused the initial population of "all known jobs" to fail for any jobs that did not produce assets.  Its okay for a job to not produce assets.  Things can still depend on that job, and the job can rerun later when some dependency is met to produce assets. This caused certain types of files, like material types, and Shader variant lists not never compile in the right order, leading to errors.
* Several Atom systems were issuing asserts when their assets were missing but not ensuring that the assets were compiled before doing so. these calls have changed into calls which ensure the asset is compiled if possible, before they try to get it and assert if missing.
* Jobs with missing source dependencies were not actually tracking when those dependencies actually appear, and were thus always executing at the very end, even if asked for the AP to prioritize them. This can happen with generated files, like material type files, which all fbx and materials can depend on, leading to a situation where all fbx files process last.
* Jobs that were cancelled were considered failed, but jobs only cancel when they are replaced by a new job to do the same thing with more information.  Becuase the jobs were considered failed, the asset processor would tell the connected game engine or editor that the job failed, which was causing certain Atom systems that were blocking on their asset to be compiled to assert even thoough the asset was actually still going to be compiled later.
* When a job is cancelled because it is replaced by a fresh job, it was not copying the priority or escalation from the old job.  This caused critical assets to move to the bottom of the list.
* Common jobs are jobs that emit new assets and thus new jobs.  Those jobs need to be prioritized to run with a high priority so that other jobs can reorder depending on the results.
* It was possible to get into a deadlock if a builder asked to sync- compile an asset.  This is because the builder is part of the asset processor itself, and its not good for them to block waiting for any
  reason.   Added a config entry for allowing such blocking sync requests,
  and set it to false by default in builders.

With all of these changes, AP now builds all assets with no failures appearing in the middle of the compilation.  You can now start the editor while your cache is empty and load a level like sponza immediately, without waiting for ALL the assets to process, and expect no errors.

(You will instead see placeholder assets until they are replaced by the real assets, which escalate to the top of the list automatically).


## How was this PR tested?

* A lot of opaque-box-testing, for example, deleting the cache, starting Editor with no cache, entering levels ASAP and trying to induce asserts or crashes.
* A lot of transparent-box-testing, that is, examining innards, logs, breakpoints, making sure the job dump looks good, adding traceprintfs, etc.
* CI Build auto tests
* AP Auto tests
* compiling assets for android, pc, etc,  from completely scratch and making sure 0 errors emit.

pull_requests.title: Fix crash after resuming Android build
pull_requests.body:
## What does this PR do?

Android application creates MainWindow on APP_CMD_INIT_WINDOW command and destroys it on APP_CMD_TERM_WINDOW command.
When an android app goes into background on APP_CMD_PAUSE command then its execution is stopped via androidEnv->SetIsRunning(false); and the MainWindow is destroyed on APP_CMD_TERM_WINDOW command.

When the app resumes the first command is  APP_CMD_START, then APP_CMD_RESUME and only after that APP_CMD_INIT_WINDOW. 
Currently an execution is restarted on APP_CMD_RESUME via androidEnv->SetIsRunning(true) but the MainWindow is not recreated yet and any code of the application that tries to use it without checking for nullptr will crash.
So the fix is to move androidEnv->SetIsRunning(true) from APP_CMD_RESUME command to APP_CMD_GAINED_FOCUS one.

## How was this PR tested?

At least 2 different android applications that 100% crashed after resuming now continue successfully.

pull_requests.title: Updates miniaudio to latest
pull_requests.body:
## What does this PR do?

1. Adds a function to 3rdparty.cmake to handle 3rd party fetchcontent libraries, following DRY principle
2. Updates miniaudio to latest stable release.
3. Ensures that the vorbis plugin is installed and running
4. Ensures that the node plugins for miniaudio are included in the install.

Note that miniaudio used to be a header-only library and used stb's "Single header file" vorbis to provide ogg/vorbis support.

The latest miniaudio is a real full static library and header, and uses the real vorbis and ogg.  While miniaudio doesn't force you to invoke cmake `install` commands, vorbis and ogg do, so we had to set the EXCLUDE_FROM_ALL property for them, which prevents their `install` commands from executing.  This also prevents them from being added to the dependency tree at all, so `add_dependencies` had to be invoked.

Other than that, this is a pretty standard 3rd party fetchcontent situation, and shoudl serve as a model for how to do others like this.

Should also fix the following issues
https://github.com/o3de/o3de/issues/18263
https://github.com/o3de/o3de/issues/17600

Note that I also include the optional miniaudio nodes (reverb, for example).  Miniaudio gem doesn't use them yet, but since they are now being built and included, it *could* if someone wants to hook them up in the node graph or whatever.

I'm actually not sure how to use a miniaudio plugin, like the vorbis plugin, but its being built at least.

EDIT:  I figured out how to use and add the vorbis plugin, initialize it, and test it on linux, windows and android.

# How was this PR tested?

I tested it in editor in debug and profile.

I also compiled in release, and built a game project with it in debug, profile,release, and also `monolithic` release to ensure no linkage issues.

I also did the same tests on linux.

I repeated the test on profile Android, I don't have the ability to do a full monolithic release test on android, but the sound does play, even the plugin vorbis file, in profile android.

I ran across this problem when implementing a [path tracing gem](https://github.com/mapret/O3DEPathTracing) (repository not really ready for public consumption yet), which uses emissive geometry as light source. ## What does this PR do?

I was trying to benchmark the streamer stuff to compare it for the new PR that improves it, and found these benchmarks to be horribly broken and full of memory problems.

After these fixes, you can run the benchmarks in debug or profile, ASAN or not ASAN, and it will run to the end.

Note that the majority of these changes are releated to benchmark threading - that is, you can run a benchmark with multiple threads.  If you do, it creates ONE of your fixtures, but invokes SetUp repeatedly from different threads, passing in a `state` object that indicates the thread index (0 to n).  

The tests didn't seem to understand this and were doing global teardown and creation on every thread instead of just thread 0, things like creating and destroying global objects like the allocators.

Running the benchmarks, compiling and running the CI build.

pull_requests.title: Introduce OpenParticleSystem
pull_requests.body:
## What does this PR do?

This PR contributes an open particle system to the O3DE. It is a standalone Gem. It contains a particle runtime, a particle editor, particle asset and its builder.

### Particle runtime

The particle system supports 3 types of particles: sprite particle, ribbon particle and mesh particle. Each type of particles supports kinds of velocity, speed, forces. The logic of creating and using the particles are more or less similar to what in Unreal Engine or Unity.

The gem itself has some particle samples, one can add a particle entity in the level and open a sample particle to take a try.

### Particle editor

The particle editor can create or modify the particles, it can open multiple particles at one time. You can add multiple emitters for each particle in it.

Also, the particles are saved as .particle files, there is a corresponding particle asset builder.

### Possible improvements

From what we see, this particle system has the following things to do, that's one of the reasons we open source it, we would like the community to work together to improve it.

#### 1. GPU particle

Currently, all particle movements are calculated in CPU, which would cause performance issue if you have large scale particles. Actually some of the works can be GPU driven.

#### 2. Refactoring of particle simulation core.

The rendering of the particle, i.e., the draw calls are sent to forward/transparent pass to draw the particles, it is more or less following the engine's draw procedure and using the engine's infrastructure. However, the movement update, it used to be a separated library (now the `SimuCore`), so most of the mathematic calculation were done by a duplicated abstraction of math lib, they can actually use the math library from `AzCore`, I managed to replace some of them, but some others are still there.

#### 3. Complex physical collision

Currently the particle can only have physical collision to a plane, it's very simple. Colliding with meshes and actors requires some changes in the engine to get the shape and position. Ideally it should be able to colliding with meshes, actors, waters(if we have) and terrain.

#### 4. Particle logic and editor enhancement

It's never to say a particle system is flexible enough to use, comparing to Unreal or Unity's particle system, we are still lack of some parts. The following are some parts we see:

- Supports manual movement track, by manual curve.
- Maximum/minimum values supported by curve.
- Particle events open to the engine
- Particle supports timeline preview
- Preview of particle material
- One-key reset default value
- Copy-paste of float3 values
- Auto save of particle documents.

#### 5. Multi-material support for particles

Currently the mesh particle only shading in standard pbr's way, would be good if we can add multiple material support for that.

### Known issues

A particle system is actually very complex, so I have to say there are some still some issues we are not able to fix before open sourcing it. The storage of the particle curve and random values sometimes might not work properly, actually the particle document format can be refactored to be more stable.

pull_requests.description: This PR contributes an open particle system to the O3DE. It is a standalone Gem. It contains a particle runtime, a particle editor, particle asset and its builder.

pull_requests.title: Add basic support for CLAS (Cluster-level acceleration structure) in Vulkan
pull_requests.body:
## What does this PR do?

This PR adds basic support for using Cluster-level acceleration structures (CLAS) with the Vulkan RHI.

CLAS is part of [Nvidia RTX MegaGeoemtry](https://developer.nvidia.com/blog/nvidia-rtx-mega-geometry-now-available-with-new-vulkan-samples/), which was introduced earlier this year, and is an additional layer in the ray tracing acceleration structure hierarchy. By building bottom-level acceleration structures (BLAS) from CLASes instead of triangle meshes, the total acceleration structure build and update time can be reduced.

Instead of creating a number of CPU-based BLAS structs, with this method the developer only needs to supply an indirect buffer of `RayTracingClasBuildTriangleClusterInfo` instances the the RHI, which can (and should be) assembled on the GPU, meaning that far less calls to the Vulkan API and CPU work is required.

There are two accompanying samples to this PR in the AtomSampleViewer:
* An [RHI sample](https://github.com/o3de/o3de-atom-sampleviewer/pull/715) shows how the RHI API can be used: This minimal sample only shows how the new API can be used, and does note really show the strengths of this implementation.
* A [Performance sample](https://github.com/o3de/o3de-atom-sampleviewer/pull/716) shows how the RPI API can be used and how ray tracing performance for animated meshes can be improved by using CLAS. This is similar to [vk_animated_clusters](https://github.com/nvpro-samples/vk_animated_clusters?tab=readme-ov-file#preliminary-performance-results) from Nvidia, but only with simple shapes as at the time of writing this sample the meshoptimizer library, which would be required for clustering meshes, was not yet in the engine.

Detailled changes:
* Update vulkan.h header to include `VK_NV_cluster_acceleration_structure`: Rebuild the vulkan.h file with the glad python script (There are many unrelated whitespace changes in the file, it seems the python script uses the indentation of the type/name elements in the source xml file of the Vulkan spec to eg. indent the generated struct definitions, which seems really sloppy). This change alone lead to almost half of the added and almost all of the removed lines (+1410 -1214).
* Add RPI+RHI function to get the device address of a GPU buffer or buffer view (`uint64_t GetDeviceAddress()`): This makes handling vertex and index data more flexible than supplying a CPU StreamBufferView struct, since each CLAS can have a different vertex and index buffer address, and multiple CLASes are built with each command from an indirect buffer
* Add an expanded and compacted azsl equivalent of the `VkClusterAccelerationStructureBuildTriangleClusterInfoNV` struct: These are used to populate the indirect command buffer for the build-BLAS command, and since azsl does not allow to define the number of bits for integer members in structs, this "compaction" has to be done manually
* Request the `VK_NV_CLUSTER_ACCELERATION_STRUCTURE_EXTENSION` to be loaded, query the device for support of this feature and set the `RHI::DeviceFeatures::m_rayTracingClas` flag
* Allocate required buffers and call Vulkan commands: For now, only the "Implicit destinations" mode is supported: In this mode, the implementation sub-allocates data for each built cluster acceleration structure from a supplied dstImplicitData buffer (With "Explicit destinations" the destination address of each built CLAS would be supplied by the developer)
* Add new fields `m_clusterBlasDescriptor`/`m_clusterOffsetBufferView` in `RayTracingFeatureProcessorInterface`: These fields supply the configuration and buffers for CLAS usage and allow for the same API of `AddMesh`/`RemoveMesh` to be used for cluster meshes from the RPI API. `RayTracingAccelerationStructurePass` and `RayTracingSceneUtils.azsli` were updated as well to be able to handle clusters. The `RayTracingSubMeshBufferFlags::ClusterGeometryBuffers` option is useful if not all clusters of a build operation are in the same GPU buffer, since in the `VkClusterAccelerationStructureBuildTriangleClusterInfoNV` struct the position and index buffers are supplied by GPU addresses (and it does not matter in how many buffers the cluster data is stored in), but in the engine hit shaders geometry data is accessed by bindless read indices, which are specific to a BufferView.

Future work:
* DX12 support: As of now, the d3d12.h file of the Windows SDK does not yet contain the new structs and enums required for implementing this feature (eg. `D3D12_RAYTRACING_ACCELERATION_STRUCTURE_MULTI_INDIRECT_TRIANGLE_CLUSTER_ARGS`). Once the headers are updated, the minimum reuqired Windows SDK version would also need to be updated (as well as the CI build nodes), or the clas-specific code would need to be disabled by some `#ifdef`s (same as the `AZ_DX12_DXR_SUPPORT` macro), or would require an additional library like NVRHI to get access to these new data types.
* Vulkan validation layers: The new structs and enums of this feature are flagged as unknown by the Vulkan validation, which leads to unnecessary validation errors. The `vulkan-validationlayers` 3rd-party package would need to be updated to at least version 1.4.307 to fix this.

## How was this PR tested?

**Important for testing**: The current DXC version cannot supply the `ClusterIDNV` builtin variable, which is required for getting the proper per-cluster vertex data (position, uv, ...) in hit shaders (visualizing barycentrics, Triangle ID, ClusterID, Instance ID work fine by default) The function `int GetClusterId()` in `RayTracingSceneUtils.azsli` contains the proper implementation, but it is not enabled by default since it would lead to shader compile errors. A newer version of DXC is required for this function to work properly (which means building it from source; eg. DXC release-1.8.2505.1-o3de + updating the DXC supmodules `SPIRV-Tools` and `SPIRV-Headers` to latest; or straight up using the DXC main branch (The reason for this is that there was a change required in [SPIRV-Tools](https://github.com/KhronosGroup/SPIRV-Tools/pull/6163) (which is a dependency of DXC), which did not make it to the 1.8.2505.1 release). Due to the inherent security concerns of running unverified binary files I will not share the DXC binaries I used for implementing this PR, but testers would have to build it themselves (at least until the next DXC release)).
Furthermore, a fairly recent GPU driver is needed to support the CLAS feature at all.

This code is not used by the engine itself, but there are the following two samples:
* [RHI Sample](https://github.com/o3de/o3de-atom-sampleviewer/pull/715)
* [Performance sample](https://github.com/o3de/o3de-atom-sampleviewer/pull/716)

pull_requests.title: Fix AssetProcessor getting stuck with assets still pending
pull_requests.body:
## What does this PR do?

Assets that depend on other assets that never appear, either becuase the dependency is cyclic, or becuase they are simply missing, were stuck at the end, and never started, would wait for their dependencies forever.

An example of this is the new OpenParticle gem declared that` .particle` depended on `Atom Material Builder` but the name of the material builder was instead `Material Builder`.  So these jobs stuck in pending forever and never processed. 

This unblocks them by processing them anyway, even though they are missing their dependencies.  This allows the individual builder for those assets to react appropriately, for example, showing errors and so on, listing problems, instead of not even trying.

It unblocks the jobs one at a time, in case there is a cyclic dependency. They will still process last, just in case the file they were waiting for appears.

## How was this PR tested?

Live testing, using the OpenParticle PR which 100% reproduced the issue.

After applying this fix, the AP displayed the warning message, and unblocked those files.

pull_requests.description: Assets that depend on other assets that never appear, either becuase the dependency is cyclic, or becuase they are simply missing, were stuck at the end, and never started, would wait for their dependencies forever.

pull_requests.title: Set initial position in hinge articulation within limits; Add IsRootArticulation to bus
pull_requests.body:
## What does this PR do?

There are two main changes in this PR in the `ArticulationLilnk` Component, solving problems or possible problems. Both did exist before, but it is better to solve the issue in 2510 than wait for another release. The changes are small and are not likely to introduce new issues.

First, a logic to set a hinge joint position to be within the limits was added. 
Although the names of variables in serialized context suggest otherwise (_positive_ and _negative_ limit),  we allow any limits for hinge joints in articulation joints. E.g., it is possible to set up a hinge joint operating between -30 and -10 deg. The original code would create a joint initialized at 0 deg. At the first tick of the physics, the joint would be forced to the operating limits, getting a momentum that might influence other dynamic bodies. This change sets the initial position to min+epsilon or max-epsilon (depending on the limits) before the physics starts. In the optimal solution, the initial value could be selected by the user, to ensure the rest of the joint's parameters is fulfilled, but this should be considered a feature and should be added to the `development` branch rather than `stabilization`. Note: the names of the variables in serialized context were not changed to ensure the old prefabs can load without any changes.

A second change, the addition of existing method `IsRootArticulation` to the bus, allows other parties to check if the articulation is a root. This is needed in other Gems. Currently, it is either solved by a code duplication (checking if the parent entity has the articulation link) or by checking if the articulation has the degree of freedom for the specified axis. The latter throws an error:
```
Articulation root does not have an inbound joint.
```
when called for a root articulation, which is not the best user experience. It is important to add, that the current implementation of the method should be refactored, as it causes issues in some cases (e.g. copying the component). But this will be fixed separately, on the `development` branch.

Additionally:
- one description in the edit context was fixed (copy paste error)
- few lines of code were re-formatted to follow the formatting rules (not all lines were formatted to ensure PR was not too big)

## How was this PR tested?

- built and run the code with `PhysX` and `PhysX5` Gems
- tested articulations with positive only and negative only hinge limits

An example of the first problem: _knees_ of the Spot can bend between -116 and -14 deg. The body of the robot is in fixed position and the legs are moved only by the gravity (motors are off).

Before:
![spot_before](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/2495b5ab-8770-4b66-a0e1-d27b3ec14dba)

After:
![spot_after](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0e4638fb-7c9a-43c6-8491-517a404b1917)

pull_requests.title: Deferred Fullscreen Draw Items
pull_requests.body:
## What does this PR do?

This PR replaces [PR 18740](https://github.com/o3de/o3de/pull/18740), since that PR had several impactful changes that were merged in separate PRs since.

This PR adds the `DeferredMaterialFeatureProcessor`, that generates and manages fullscreen draw items for unique combinations of material-type and shader-options based on mesh-materials in the scene.

The functionality can be controlled with the `r_deferredMaterialRenderingEnabled` cvar, and is disabled by default.

The draw-items are generated only for material shader-items with `DrawItemType::Deferred`, and they use the same `DrawListTag` - functionality as the other Draw-Items, and are therefore executed by a normal `RasterPass` with the matching tag. 

Although the input-assembly is set up for a single fullscreen triangle, and the draw-item assumes that the shader-code for the vertex-shader is also set up accordingly.

Note that this does not add a new render pipeline, and updates only a few shader files: The deferred render pipeline is demonstrated in the Atom Sample Viewer, in the RPI->Mesh - sample, as a new render pipeline type, in [ASV PR 700](https://github.com/o3de/o3de-atom-sampleviewer/pull/700).
 
## How was this PR tested?
This was tested with the Atom Sample viewer on windows and vulkan.

Results from the sample: This is a material-canvas material that writes a color based on the world-position into the emissive channel, which doesn't work with the old deferred pipeline, since that only evaluates the base-color of the material.

**default (forward+) pipeline:**
![small_default_pipeline_material_canvas](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/43e34f7d-c2dc-4f5c-9f40-771bdaa4d81e)
**old deferred pipeline:**
![small_old_deferred-pipeline_material_canvas](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0cec9a0c-19d9-4090-9dc1-dbf82baf3b12)
**new deferred pipeline:**
![small_new_deferred_pipeline_material_canvas](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/cc6b7d55-3bff-495b-9124-9ff21c4db086)

pull_requests.title: Fix crash when accessing unbound textures in materials
pull_requests.body:
## What does this PR do?

Potential fix for [PR 19208](https://github.com/o3de/o3de/issues/19208)

When a material accesses a texture that is not bound, it currently accesses whatever texture is bound at bindless read index 0.
If there is nothing bound, vulkan systems are likely to crash.

This PR explicitly binds a Null - texture and propagates the bindless read index to the materials.

Note that this required a manual re-processing of all material shaders, since changing the MaterialSRG - files did not trigger any rebuilds automatically.

## How was this PR tested?

Vulkan / DX12 on Windows only. I was not able to reproduce the linked issue, since that occurs only on linux.

pull_requests.title: Replaces name fields of assets in Particle with Asset References
pull_requests.body:
## What does this PR do?

The product file data (as in, the compiled asset) was always using asset references (`AZ::Data::Asset<Material> etc`) but the source json files were using string path names like `"material" = "Atom/Examples/Blah.material"`.

This made it much more expensive at compile time and validation time, for example, if you assign "Sphere.azmodel" it was previously trying to make sure that "sphere.fbx" was compiled,by replacing the extension "azmodel" with "fbx" and establishing a dependency on that.  However, its possible that "sphere.azmodel" came from "sphere.obj", or "sphere.usd", or any other model format.

It's also possible that "sphere.azmodel" came from a model that was not named "sphere" at all, for example, if you had a model named "shapes.fbx", that contained a cube, and a sphere.  There simply is not enough information in a product file name alone to know what file to compile or depend on to produce it.  (Same goes for materials, since they could be generated ones).

This change makes it so that the source files (`.particle` files) also use Asset References in json, which means that the Asset Processor can directly validate that the asset exists, and that it is of the correct type, and does not need to do any guessing based on file names.   It makes the compile job only depend on the material (and it only depends on that for validating that the material type is ok, which is something we could probably revisit).  It does not load the model at any point during the job anymore, as all it needs is the ID, not the asset data.

It also replaces the custom asset control used in the ComboBox widget, with the standard `AZ::ToolsFramework` asset line control and picker, as well as uses the tools busses to convert from the old "name" format to the new "asset reference" format, so all old assets still function as before.

In order to convert them, I needed to make 2 changes to the core: the first is there was simply a bug where the API to look up Asset products by the source file name - the function was not actually filling out the entire response structure, leaving the file name empty.

The second is a new API convenience function to do a best guess conversion of a file name to an asset reference, by doing a series of lookups from the fastest most reliable to the slowest "Guess".  Given a file name of either a source or product, it can do its best to find what the thing is looking for.  This is used when loading the particle json file, if it discovers the old format.

## How was this PR tested?

* Loading the old data into the editor and the runtime, after these changes, to make sure the old data still worked.
* Rebuilding all the assets in asset processor, loading the new processed assets, making sure no issues with the asset jobs.
* Altering, and then saving the data, so that it would be the new format, then repeating the above two tests.
* Renaming an emitter, changing its materials, meshes, skeleton mesh, then repeating the first two bullet points.

pull_requests.title: Add Windows platform tab with Graphics API and validation settings
pull_requests.body:
## What does this PR do?
This PR introduces a new tab widget dedicated to **Windows**-specific platform settings 
in the ProjectSettingsTool (`Editor->File->Edit Platform Settings->Windows`).

It adds configuration options for Graphics API selection and validation mode, 
which are persisted to the `rhi.setreg` file.

- Introduced a dedicated tab for Windows platform settings.

- Added a dropdown allowing users to select either **DX12** or **Vulkan** as the preferred graphics API.

- Introduced a dropdown to set the validation mode, with options:

        Disabled
        Enabled (warnings and errors)
        Verbose (warnings, errors, and info)
        GPU

New settings are encapsulated in `WindowsGraphics`, part of the `WindowsSettings` class.
    Settings are serialized via Reflect() and exposed in the editor UI using combo boxes.
    `SaveRHISettings()` writes the selected configuration to `rhi.setreg` at:
    (**<user_project>/Registry/rhi.setreg**)

Example:
```
{
    "O3DE": {
        "Atom": {
            "RHI": {
                "FactoryManager": {
                    "factoriesPriority": [
                        "Vulkan",
                        "DX12"
                    ]
                }
            },
            "rhi-device-validation": "enable"
        }
    }
}
```
This file is read at startup by both the `Editor` and `GameLauncher` to configure the RHI.

These settings are also serialized to the platform-specific project file:
**<user_project>/Platform/Windows/windows_project.json**

Example:
```
{
    "Tags": [
        "Windows"
    ],
    "windows_settings": {
        "graphics": {
            "graphicsAPI": "Vulkan",
            "validationMode": 0
        }
    }
}
```

## How was this PR tested?
_Editor Build & Launch_
Successfully built the editor with the new Windows tab widget included.
Launched the editor and navigated to the `File->Edit Platform Settings->Windows`

_Verified UI Integration_ 
Confirmed the Windows tab appears correctly alongside Android and iOS tabs
Checked that the Graphics API and Validation Mode dropdowns were visible and correctly populated.

_Changed Settings and Saved_
Selected different combinations of Graphics API (DX12/Vulkan) and Validation Mode (disable, enable, verbose, gpu). 
Saved the settings and confirmed no errors occurred during serialization.

_Inspected rhi.settings File_ 
Navigated to the location where rhi.setreg is saved. 
Verified that the selected values were correctly written.

_Restarted Editor/GameLauncher_ 
Relaunched both tools and confirmed that the RHI system initialized with the configured settings.

pull_requests.title: Adds additional unit tests for AZStd::copy_backward
pull_requests.body:
## What does this PR do?

One github actions machine failed one time with this test, in a way I can't figure out.

```
2025-10-01T19:30:13.7539471Z [ RUN      ] Algorithms.CopyBackwardFastCopy
2025-10-01T19:30:13.7540063Z E:\o3de\Code\Framework\AzCore\AzCore/std/createdestroy.h(302): error: AZStd::copy_backward memory overlaps use AZStd::copy!
2025-10-01T19:30:13.7540073Z 
2025-10-01T19:30:13.7540259Z [  FAILED  ] Algorithms.CopyBackwardFastCopy (0 ms)
```

So I've added 4 additional tests which will clarify what the situation is if it happens again, including keeping the original tests.

The 4 new tests are
* Arrays are right next to each other, but not overlapping, with dest after src
* Arrays are right next to each other, but not overlapping, with dest before src.
* Arrays are overlapping in a way that violates the C++ standard for undefined behavior (dest.end within src range)
* Arrays are overlapping but in a non-illegal way (dest.end outside src range).

The third is an "Assert test" in that it is expected to assert, and will fail if it does not.
The others are regular tests, and will fail if they assert, or do not produce the expected outputs.

see https://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/algorithm/copy_backward.html - the only UB assert is supposed to be if `dest.end` (the last param) is within `(first, last]` which is what we were already testing for.

## How was this PR tested?

Running all the tests against the existing code, both in profile and debug on windows.

No errors.  So its still a bit of a mystery why that test failed on GHA (just that one time).

pull_requests.title: Unify fast math across all platforms
pull_requests.body:
## What does this PR do?

Note to reviewers, this consists of 2 commits, the first one is the changes to the cmake files, the second one is the changes to the headers and cpp files, to ease review.  The changes to the cpp and header files was required for two cases
* Where we were converting from double literal (ie, `1.323` instead of `1.323f` to float - without fast math, this issues a warning on the MSVC compilers.
* Where we were using `numerical_limits<T>::infinity`, `INF`, `NAN`, `INFINITY` anywhere with fast math active, which is also a compiler warning.
* Adding the flag to disable fast math (even if fast math is on in o3de) in google test, which cannot compile with fast math enabled.

"Fast Math" was turned on for windows via `/fp:fast` and android via `-ffast-math` but not for any other platform.
Note that we don't need fast math enabled for O3DE to function and neither does it actually make much of a difference in our actual performance - all the math heavy sections of O3DE for example use SIMD, NEON, or other specialized custom instructions that are unaffected by "fast math" anyway.  

This PR turns "Fast math" off by default, but still allows you to turn it on, if you want, via a cmake option.

When fast math is on, a special compiler flag is added across all platforms that you can check to see if its enabled or not. 

Note that when fast math IS enabled, the concept of Infinity and NaN no longer make sense, that is, when fast math is enabled, division by zero, multiplication of two incredibly overflow large numbers, double/float overflows, double/float underflows, square roots of negatives, and any other thing that the floating point system cannot deal with just work "anyway" and produce undefined behavior, without raising flags, becoming NaN or inf or anything.  You cannot compare anything with `infinity` or `INFINITY` or NaN or anything like that when fast math is enabled, because those concepts don't exist anymore and operations that would result in those values no longer do.

## How was this PR tested?

Compile and run under
* Windows MSVC2019  (fast math off and on)   + Running CI Build tests locally, with and without  fast math
* Windows MSVC2022 (fast math off and on)    + Running CI Build tests locally, with and without fast math
* Clang 20 MSVCRT vc2022 (fast math off and on)
* Clang 18 MSVCRT vc2019 (fast math off and on)
* Clang 14 MSVCRT vc2019 (fast math off and on)
* Android Clang 20 (fast math off and on) on a real device.

pull_requests.title: Feature: Additional Tonemapping Operators
pull_requests.body:
## What does this PR do?

Implements AgX tonemapping based on Google Filament's 7th order polynomial sigmoid approximation.
- Default AgX style is fairly low contrast compared to other tonemappers like ACES. Color grading should be adjusted.
- Punchy AgX style introduces some contrast and saturation into the tonemapper, bringing it closer to an "out of the box" look.
- Golden AgX style applies a very heavy golden tint to the scene. May have limited usefulness, but most implementations include this anyways.
- Warm AgX is my own tuned look with a small bump to contrast and a much more subtle warm tint.

Implements Khronos PBR Neutral tonemapping.
- [View this press release for an overview and motivations](https://www.khronos.org/news/press/khronos-pbr-neutral-tone-mapper-released-for-true-to-life-color-rendering-of-3d-products)
- Good for Archviz, product rendering etc, but generally looks pleasant in most scenes I've tested.

Improves basic Reinhard tonemapping with a luminance aware "extended" implementation.
- [View the reference and theory here](https://64.github.io/tonemapping/#extended-reinhard-luminance-tone-map)
- Should improve the general look of scenes using this tonemapper, regaining some lost saturation.

## How was this PR tested?

Only tested on Linux so far. Doesn't do anything drastic with the engine, mostly just introducing some enums and some new shader code.

pull_requests.title: Fail shader build job if azsl preprocessing fails
pull_requests.body:
## What does this PR do?

This PR changes the AssetProcessor behavior such that missing includes are treated as job failure. Before that, a missing include lead to an error in the event log, but shader processing continued, and only failed if the missing include leads to further compile errors. This behavior does not seem reasonable, and can lead to errors when an azsl file also works without a specific include but the behavior is changed by the include (eg. by using `#if defined(...)` or overwriting a macro definition).

## How was this PR tested?

Run the AssetProcessor with clean cache for a small test project, the AutomatedTesting project, and the AtomSampleViewer, all projects processed without showing an error. Adding an invalid `#include` to an azsl or azsli file marks the affected shader assets as failed in the job list.

pull_requests.title: Updates the android project generator for latest (Gradle 8.13, AGP 8.13, JDK 17)
pull_requests.body:
## What does this PR do?

This updates the o3de `android-generate` and `androic-configure` commands to handle the latest versions of JDK, Gradle, NDK, and the Android Gradle Plugin.  It also corrects the assignment syntax of various settings in the gradle file, to use the `=` operator instead of a space, which is deprecated syntax.

The following versions are now supported (these are working combinations)
* Android Gradle Plugin v8.13, Gradle v8.13, JDK 17
* Android Gradle Plugin v8.12, Gradle v8.13, JDK 17
* Android Gradle Plugin v8.11, Gradle v8.13, JDK 17

This essentially means that if you install android studio, and just use the latest everything, it works.

Note also that gradle deprecated space assignment for properties, that is, 
```gradle
   somenamespace {
      blahproperty 5
  }
```
is not legal, it should be `blahproperty = 5`

Note that function calls still use spaces.  I fixed these up too, where I could find them, and this results in the "generate project" function not emitting any errors or warnings anymore.  Actually building it still generates some warnings, but those warnings are from libraries provided by google (ie, the android play store stuff), so its kind of up to them to fix those.

## How was this PR tested?

Building, testing and running on actual android hardware (pixel 8) with the above NDKs, JDKs, Gradle, AGP.

Following the O3DE https://www.docs.o3de.org/docs/user-guide/platforms/android/generating_android_project_windows guide, and setting the versions in the config as specified.

pull_requests.title: Entity Driven Startup and Hierarchical Activation Handling
pull_requests.body: # Updated Info Have a foundation of EntityActiveTypes and Generic Entity Activation Handling. Have a foundation for Hierarchical Activation Handling. I've created some Discord Support Threads to focus on testing and benchmarking planning. Automated Testing: https://discord.com/channels/805939474655346758/1433271756470354162 Benchmarking: https://discord.com/channels/805939474655346758/1433269699298332822 # What does this PR do? Resolving this Feature Request: https://github.com/o3de/o3de/issues/18840 This PR introduces Hierarchical (De)Activation along with introducing a fully functioning "Start Inactive" feature. ## Entity State Management Firstly, I extended the Entity Class to enable local state data storage, and state change logic. - By introducing startActive, localActive, and parentActive, I can evaluate the "effective active state", and handle the changing of state directly on the entity. - Using SetParentActive, or SetLocalActive, I change the stored states, then evaluate for the effective state. - Evaluate Active state checks for the active state, and if the current standing status of the Entity is correct, it calls Activate and Deactivate accordingly. - Newly Inactive, while State::Active allows for Deactivation. - Newly Active, while State::Init allows for Activation. ## Hierarchical (De)Activation I then extend the featureset into the GameEntityContext class. By monitoring the addition, removal, and parent changing of the context owned entities I can maintain an active hierarchy tree of Entities relationships to eachother. By doing this I remove the need to use the TransformBus to access the Parent -> Child relationships at Activation time. I use some set up methods laced around the AddEntity and DestroyEntity methods to update the tree over time, and then using `GetEntityTreeFromRootEntity` to access a sub-tree of the main tree starting from the root entity, I can loop over in ascending or descending order to properly handle the activation in the right order necessary to preserve functionality. I have a few utility classes added that can be removed and instead added into the methods themselves, or preserved and used across the rest of the class to homogenize things like "FindEntity". #### Testing the Activation + Entity State (De)Activation successfully starts at the root entity and handles the state change in the order necessary to preserve functionality. Changing a child state during a parent inactive state preserves the hierarchichal status, i.e. it remains inactive. But upon activation, it also preserves the childs state. Allowing for any manipulation of entity states at any point in the hierarchy to maintain it's parent -> child status, regardless of parent, child, or childs descendants states. https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3e67f4fc-c90c-4f4d-9f30-66bb7b7e7899 The Script being used: #### Possible loose ends 1) The EntityContext has a "HandleEntityRemoved" event that is not present on the GameEntityContext. I would need to override that to include the "remove from tree" method for the entity if there's any possibility the runtime entities can be removed from the context without destruction, which is already handled. 2) Default Activate, and Deactivate, do not take any part in the new Entity State Handling, therefore, anything still relying on that directly will not be able to drive any changes to entity effective state. ## OnParentChanged Tree Maintenance I have submitted the PR with this remaining as an issue. Unfortunately there's a lot going on in the TransformComponent that nullifies being able to parent a child to a deactivated parent, or for parenting an inactive child. In the GameEntityContext I am listening to the many entity OnParentChanged events to reconfigure the tree. Then process how the new childs state should be. However, due to disparities in how the transform handles its data it fails to properly integrate unless both entities are active at ReParent time. Which happens BEFORE my tree can react and process it properly. #### Evaluating the current TransformComponent functionality Current TransformComponent assumes it is Active. Because it is connected to many busses it needs to handle transform and parent changes, due to the source of the info coming from events from other entities. m_parentTM gets nulled when parent becomes deactivated. (event) m_parentActive is also made false. When calling SetParent: - SetParentImpl will get new parent entity if valid - Will register to parent busses, including parent TransformNotificationBus, and parent TransformHierarchyInformationBus (Because it assumes its active at the time of processing.) - It then calls OnParentChanged to self notification handler. - It then calls parent OnChildAdded - It then calls NotifyChildChangedEvent to the parent transform. - The only place I can find a set new transform is on: OnTransformChangedImpl - Without the m_parentTM it ignores any new TransformChanges which are coming from the parent. - I can only assume that a preliminary TransformChange happens when the ChildChanged event fires. #### Logical Needs Active -> Active already fine. Active -> Inactive - Needs to properly register as child to parent, parent needs to be able to listen and respond. - Needs to receive proper transform changes - This would also require sending transform changes down through the descendants. - Context then Steps in and it needs to deactivate it's children up to itself, knowing that upon it's de-activation it's already properly configured to the inactive parents transform state. Inactive -> Active - Needs to process receiving its own events and changes as it messages the parent events. - Needs to receive proper transform changes. - This would also require sending transform changes down through the descendants. - Context then Steps in and activates it from top to bottom of its hierarchy, knowing that the root is properly configured to the active aprents transform state. Inactive -> Inactive - Needs to send and receive events, parent needs to send and receive events. - Needs to receive proper transform changes. - This would also require sending transform changes down through the descendants. - The active state has not changed, therefore, no state changes need to be made. #### Reparenting Conclusion Due to it anticipating that a Deactivated Parent will not respond to events, and due to it requiring incoming events during various stages of its transform handling, I think one of the easiest ways to enable more varied handling would be to change how/when the Transform Component decides to connect and disconnect from busses. I think it's a valid thought as unlike basically every other component, transform is a very necessary representation of the physical presence of any/every entity in world space. If we can assure that a child entity successfully transforms to the new parent, and then handle the new changes to state, I think we can solve this. There is a placeholder "SetGameEntityParent" event on the Game Entity Context in order to circumvent the ebusses like the activation stuff, however, depending on how we handle the TransformComp it might not be necessary. ## Start (In)Active Hijacking the former m_isRuntimeActiveByDefault variable and processing all throughout various systems: - EntitySerailizer - EntityPropertyEditor - EditorInfoRemover - SliceCompilation I have replaced the logic of the "Start Active" inspector toggle to properly impose boolean changes to the Entity::m_startActive variable. During certain cloning executions I replaced RuntimeActiveByDefault checks and sets with Get and Set StartActive utility methods. I added some guard rails to when certain hierarchichal and activation steps are called in the new active State system. Assuring that certain things aren't fired during initialization. - One of which is OnParentChanged being called during Component Init(), which happens before the Entity fully Init's. - Thankfully, this still updates the tree, making it continually reconfigure with every new transform change. Finally, I add one additional stage to the OnContextEntitiesAdded method, which handles the Init and Activation steps for all added entities to the context. Assuming that the incoming list of entities is in top down order (As activation can't happen any other way), I quickly update the Parent State of each entity from the parent state already updated in the ParentChild Tree. Lastly, instead of checking for "m_isRuntimeActiveByDefault" and then calling Activate after, I call Evaluate, which either activates itself based on its new effective state, or remains inert due to already being inactive at the time of processing. #### Testing Start Inactive I simply toggle the active on start toggle in the inspector, and then start play. Then with my previous toggle buttons, I activate the entities for the first time in play, and they work just fine! https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9bc83cbf-2d88-4a20-ac26-b88775689627 ## Depreciation I have marked all the things with "To Depreciate" or @depreciate with the date 2025/10/23 In Entity.h and .cpp I've marked the RuntimeActiveByDefault variables and methods to depreciate. The only remaining code with RuntimeActiveByDefault is in: - SliceGameEntityOwnerhipService - NetworkEntityModel - EditorEntityModel - EntityOutlinerListModel - SimpleStateComponent # Conclusion I think this is much of it tackled, I am already very excited about what it's unlocking in functionality. I have left a lot of Print logs and Comments to fully articulate what I'm doing, as well as show function order in the console to identify exactly where certain functionality interacts with everything in the start and activation process. I will obviously be happy to prune everything but the essential stuff by final submit. I would greatly appreciate any ideas of where this is not touching on which could destabilize the functionality, as well as ideas on how to tackle the one major loose end of the Transform Parenting logic. Thanks for your review!

pull_requests.title: Fix deviceIndex for DiffuseProbeGridVisualizationPreparePass
pull_requests.body:
## What does this PR do?

Fixes a multi-device problem in DiffuseProbeGrid:

Most passes have an invalid device index by default. This is correct behaviour and important for supporting e.g. alternate frame rendering (see `AFRFeatureProcessor`).
Unfortunately the invalid device index cannot be used to get device objects from multi device objects. The `DiffuseProbeGridVisualizationPreparePass` accesses some device objects through the device index from the `ScopeProducer`. This leads to a problem because the device index of the `ScopeProducer` is `InvalidDeviceIndex`.

This PR fixes this problem by assigning the `DefaultDeviceIndex` to the `ScopeProducer` of a pass if the pass has an `InvalidDeviceIndex`

## How was this PR tested?

I tested this this in the `main` branch instead of development because I get this error when running this in current development:
```
(CommandList::Submit) - SRGs slots requested by shader       provided by render item  
(CommandList::Submit) -  Slot [0]: RayTracingGlobalSrg       RayTracingGlobalSrg      
(CommandList::Submit) -  Slot [1]: RayTracingSceneSrg        RayTracingSceneSrg       
(CommandList::Submit) -  Slot [2]: RayTracingMaterialSrg     None                     
(CommandList::Submit) -  Slot [3]: None                      None                     
(CommandList::Submit) -  Slot [4]: None                      None                     
(CommandList::Submit) -  Slot [5]: None                      None                     
(CommandList::Submit) -  Slot [6]: None                      None                     
(CommandList::Submit) -  Slot [7]: Bindless                  Bindless                 
(CommandList::Submit) - Notes:
(CommandList::Submit) -  - This may be inaccurate for merged SRGs.
(CommandList::Submit) -  - This does not track SRGs occupying the same slot.

The provided SRGs don't match the SRGs requested by the shader.
```
I think the Diffuse Probe Grid shaders need to be adapted for this PR: https://github.com/o3de/o3de/pull/19123
And probably also for PR: https://github.com/o3de/o3de/pull/19218 (This adds strides to the MeshInfo struct)

I also found a another problem when testing this is the main branch: PR https://github.com/o3de/o3de/pull/19175 is needed, otherwise an assert in `DeviceResource::SetFrameAttachment` is triggered.

With these fixes the Diffuse Probe Grid doesn't crash the engine anymore, but there is still a problem where the probes don't get any radiance as described here: https://discordapp.com/channels/805939474655346758/816043793576886273/1433136399992750281
I assume the DDGI shaders need to be adapted due to some other changes in shader includes as well.

pull_requests.title: No longer require `git` to build and run in installer
pull_requests.body:
## What does this PR do?

Fixes the installer verison of O3DE so it no longer requires `git` to be installed in order to function.

The v-hacd library had 2 different files that were intended to be used, one for the code version of the engine which downloaded v-hacd from github, using git, and one for the installer version of the engine which noted the copyright information and the fact that it was being used, but didn't need to actually download it since its entire contents were subsumed inside the physx gem dlls and were not public api.

Its also only used by the tools (editor, etc) and not the standalone game runtime, so it isn't necessary to have it available in the installer build.

The original problem is caused by the use of the cmake `ly_add_external_target_path` function, which appears to make sure that the files in that location are copied to the installer.

Instead, in this case, we want different files in the installer than in the code version, so instead of using that macro, we add the directory to the module path directly, and then use `ly_install` to copy the other cmake file (that does not use git) to the installer.

## How was this PR tested?

Manual verification - making sure the correct file was copied into the installer by looking at the file that was copied.  Previously, the file that fetches v-hacd from git was included.  Now the file that just uses the existing one is included.

Manual verification - building a custom installer and using it on a project.

## What does this PR do?

This PR fixes that by copying the Depth aspect if a given image has one.

pull_requests.title: BugFix PropertyHandlerDirectory didn't work in the game project
pull_requests.body:
## What does this PR do?

PropertyHandlerDirectory is only used in one place within O3DE: the LyShine Gem's UiImageSequenceComponent.h.

**The Problem:**
The issue was that if you used the UiImageSequenceComponent and referenced a sequence located within a Gem (e.g., Gems/LyShineExamples/Assets/UI/Textures/LyShineExamples/imagesequence), everything worked correctly. However, if you copied that same sequence into the game project's Assets folder, the component could no longer find it. This was because the relative directory path was being generated incorrectly.

**The Solution:**
To fix this, I modified PropertyAssetCtrl.cpp to return an absolute path to the folder. Then, within PropertyHandlerDirectory, this absolute path is converted to the correct relative path.
As a result, the component now works correctly with assets located in both Gems and the main game project.

## How was this PR tested?

1. To test everything, I copied the directory Gems/LyShineExamples/Assets/UI/Textures/LyShineExamples/imagesequence into my game project at <MyGame>/Assets/imagesequence.
2. I opened the UI Editor and added an entity with a UiImageSequenceComponent.
3. Next, I first assigned the image sequence from the Gem path (Gems/LyShineExamples/Assets/UI/Textures/LyShineExamples/imagesequence) and verified that the component correctly recognized the sequence.
4. Then, I loaded the sequence from the game project path (<MyGame>/Assets/imagesequence) and confirmed that the component also saw it and worked correctly.

pull_requests.description: PropertyHandlerDirectory is only used in one place within O3DE: the LyShine Gem's UiImageSequenceComponent.h.

pull_requests.title: Allows you to save default paths into assetbundler rules
pull_requests.body: ## What does this PR do? AssetBundler did not actually save paths you specify in the GUI, since the intention there was that the gui is for constructing rule files that have blanks for you to fill in later, from the command line. So for example, if you made a rule that generated the delta between 2 different asset lists (by browsing for the 2 asset lists) it would not save the paths you chose. This change makes it so that the GUI actually saves those paths into the asset files, if you specify them. You can still leave them blank if you want. This would allow a smaller (1 user) team to use the GUI to assemble bundles instead of the CLI, while still allowing power users to use the command line with custom paths to substitute if they want. This does not change existing batch behavior. This also fixes an invalid param access in assetbundler, and fixes an issue where it would not properly add the `_pc`, `_android` etc warts onto the file name before trying to open them. ## How was this PR tested? Manual testing, and local CI tests (which do exercise these features). Example rules in the gui This is a delta rule, which is going to result in an asset list which only contains the difference between those two asset lists. This is useful for example if you want to make an `engine.pak` (containing all baseline assets) and then a `level01.pak` and `level02.pak` and so on which just has the level assets. you wouldn't want to include the engine assets in each level pack, so you'd create a rule to `delta(engine.assetlist, level01.assetlist)` which would result in a new list of assets in level01 but NOT the engine. You'd then turn that delta assetlist into the final level01.pak. With this change you can now do this by using the gui (click 'run rule') and it saves its state. You can still use the command line options to run this rule too, just like before, except that you can use `$` for these params to mean `just use the default value already in the file.` Note that the `$` operation was always available, the paths were just never saved into the file before. example ``` build\windows\bin\debug\AssetBundlerBatch.exe --project-path=AutomatedTesting compare --comparisonRulesFile AutomatedTesting\AssetBundling\Rules\sep.rules --platform pc --firstAssetFile=$ --secondAssetFile=$ --output=test.assetlist --print ``` example using paths instead (you'd have to do this every time, before this change) ``` build\windows\bin\debug\AssetBundlerBatch.exe --project-path=AutomatedTesting compare --comparisonRulesFile AutomatedTesting\AssetBundling\Rules\sep.rules --platform pc --firstAssetFile=AutomatedTesting\AssetBundling\AssetLists\engine.assetlist --secondAssetFile=AutomatedTesting\AssetBundling\AssetLists\automatedtesting.assetlist --output=test.assetlist --print ```

So for example, if you made a rule that generated the delta between 2 different asset lists...

pull_requests.title: Fixes several crashes in OpenParticleSystem
pull_requests.body:
## What does this PR do?

1.  Depending on random memory allocation, it was possible for the particle data pool to choose an alignment that was not SIMD aligned.  This would lead to mystery crashes on some runs and not others. Fixed by adding a custom allocator to its vector, which allocates always at least 16 byte aligned memory.
2.  Particle inheritance events were not spawning enough slots when they triggered.  This could lead to out of bounds memory access. Fixed by spawning enough slots, as well as properly calculating the slot to use when fetching inherited params.
3.  Particle builder now works without the material present.  If the material is available, it will do additional checks, but if not, it will still work.  This avoids a situation in First Time Usage where all the assets are still building, so the materials are not yet available.  If the material becomes available later, it will reprocess the particle asset, which will cause it to emit the warning as appropriate.
4.  All particles have been resaved in the new format.

## How was this PR tested?

Hand tested in Debug and Profile Windows, with **Address Sanitizer** (ASAN) enabled.  ASAN found at least one of these issues.  The other was pure luck.  I opened all the particles and ensured they functioned as before.

Please note that the resave was done by opening each particle source file in the editor, then saving them again without modifying them.   So the shuffling around of fields is something to do with the way we save JSON rather than anything I modified.  The main change is simply changing fields like `material:  blahblah.material` to a proper asset id and subid and hint field which can be looked up by uuid before the material exists on disk.

pull_requests.title: Meshlets: fix 64->32 narrowing when building IndexBufferView size
pull_requests.body:
## What does this PR do?

Fixes a build break on macOS with Xcode 26.1.1 when compiling the Meshlets gem with Xcode.

Before: Meshlets.Static failed to compile because we were passing a 64-bit
byte-size expression into RHI::IndexBufferView, which expects a 32-bit size.
With -Werror, clang treats the implicit 64->32 narrowing as a hard error.

After: Compute the byte size in 64-bit, assert it fits in 32-bit, and pass it
to IndexBufferView via an explicit cast. This keeps the intent clear and avoids
silent truncation.

## How was this PR tested?

- macOS (Xcode generator), Profile config:
  - cmake --build build/macos --config profile --target Meshlets.Static
- Also built the SDK launcher target to ensure nothing else regressed:
  - cmake --build build/macos --config profile --target O3DE_SDK

pull_requests.title: Fix Builds on macOS
pull_requests.body:
## What does this PR do?

Addresses multiple macOS build issues, with a primary focus on restoring support for generic engine builds. (i.e., when the engine is built standalone, without an accompanying project.)

Previously, the macOS project launcher CMake assumed that project-specific image resources were always present. This assumption holds for non-generic builds, but causes configuration failures for generic builds where those resources do not exist. These changes fix this by early returning from the platform launcher PAL when `launcher_generator_BUILD_GENERIC` is enabled.

In addition to the build system fix, this PR fixes other areas of drift and compilation failures on macOS:

* Updates to Atom RHI Metal code to match recent interface changes, mirroring the Vulkan RHI where appropriate
* Signature updates for unimplemented Metal ray tracing entry points to match the current RHI interfaces
* Fixes for Clang compilation errors where implicit `this` capture is no longer allowed with `=` lambda captures
* Minor type safety fixes and missing includes uncovered during macOS builds
* Cleanup of deprecated atomic initialization usage in tests

The intent is to bring macOS builds back to a functional state without changing behavior on other platforms.

## How was this PR tested?

This PR was tested on macOS 26.2 using Apple Clang 17 and CMake 4.2.

The following configurations were verified:

* Generic engine build without a game project
* Non-generic build alongside a game project

Due to the lack of a previously working macOS build, RHI Metal changes were validated primarily through successful compilation and interface consistency with Vulkan. No exhaustive runtime validation of Metal RHI behavior was performed.
pull_requests.author: EnterTheArcane
pull_requests.description: Addresses multiple macOS build issues, with a primary focus on restoring support for generic engine builds. (i.e., when the engine is built standalone, without an accompanying project.).

pull_requests.title: Initial Wayland support.
pull_requests.body:
## What does this PR do?
This adds initial support for the Wayland protocol in O3DE.
Supports switching between X11 and Wayland at runtime or compile time.

Editor support isn’t included in this PR, as it was decided to wait until Qt6, so this currently only works for GameLauncher.
I wanted to get this merged early, as it’s easier to review without the editor code involved, and it shouldn’t affect anyone if merged since `PAL_TRAIT_LINUX_WINDOW_MANAGER_WAYLAND` must be set to `ON` in CMake for it to be considered at all.

This code is functional enough from my user testing. However, if you enable Wayland in CMake and launch the Editor with the `WAYLAND_DISPLAY` environment variable defined, you will encounter issues but that's expected for now.

https://github.com/o3de/o3de/issues/18513

## Protocol usage
Wayland Protocols have licenses to them, I'm not sure of the legal side but I'll list off whats used and whats required at runtime, all protocols are required at compile time:
| Protocol | License | Required |
|--------|--------|--------|
| XDG Shell | [LINK](https://wayland.app/protocols/xdg-shell#copyright) | ✅ |
| Pointer Constraints | [LINK](https://wayland.app/protocols/pointer-constraints-unstable-v1#copyright) | ✅ |
| Relative Pointer | [LINK](https://wayland.app/protocols/relative-pointer-unstable-v1#copyright) | ✅ | 
| XDG Decoration | [LINK](https://wayland.app/protocols/xdg-decoration-unstable-v1#copyright) | ❌ |
| Cursor Shape | [LINK](https://wayland.app/protocols/cursor-shape-v1#copyright) | ❌ |
| Tablet | [LINK](https://wayland.app/protocols/tablet-v2#copyright) | ❌ |

While we are using protocols that are in staging / unstable, its not that bad, as most other Wayland applications use these as well. Mainly, it just means that if a breaking API change is made, we’ll need to update accordingly.

~~There is a small Python script under the Linux platform folder that contains the same list of protocols and is used to retrieve the build machine’s protocol copyright contents.~~ (Removed, [look at comment for details](https://github.com/o3de/o3de/pull/19477#issuecomment-3930786943))

One main concern is that we need XDG decoration support, but platforms like GNOME will *not* support decor-v1 (they may implement v2). For now, there’s a note about possibly enforcing fullscreen, as that’s the most desired behavior.

## How was this PR tested?

On my local machine with `PAL_TRAIT_LINUX_WINDOW_MANAGER_WAYLAND` defined to ON and without defining it.

## Features
- Multi-Monitor HiDPI support on Linux 
- Multi-Seat input support, if you ask to create another kb or mouse with a different player index you can access other seats. I haven't really tested this but Its on my TODO list to create a video showcasing it in the future.
- HDR output, which with my older PR's for VK HDR makes it possible :smiley:
pull_requests.author: DrogonMar
pull_requests.description: This adds initial support for the Wayland protocol in O3DE. Supports switching between X11 and Wayland at runtime or compile time.

pull_requests.title: Fix scenes with keyframes potentially applying wrong transform to submeshes
pull_requests.body:
## What does this PR do?

When importing a mesh which contains any kind of keyframes, it can under certain conditions be processed wrong (see #19431):
This can lead to the a scene graph where a mesh node is the child of a bone node, and both nodes have a transform child node with the same parameters, which leads to the same transform being applied two times when the global mesh transform is calculated.

This RP fixes this by not adding a transform node to a mesh if its immediate parent is a bone and already has a transform applied to it.

## How was this PR tested?

Add the two meshes from the linked issue to an empty scene and verified that both look the same, which is not the case in development.

Run the AssetBuilder with the `cubes-with-animation.glb` mesh from the linked issue, then print the bone, mesh, and transform nodes with some additional data from the scene graph. Currently the scene graph looks like this (The transform components under `Cube2` and `Cube2_1` both have a translation, which leads to a total translation of `-5` being applied to the mesh, leading to a larger gap between the two cubes):
```
"ROOT" IBoneData
  "Cube1" IBoneData
    "Cube1_1" IMeshData
      "transform" ITransform scale(1,1,1), translate(0,0,0)
    "transform" ITransform scale(1,1,1), translate(0,0,0)
  "Cube2" IBoneData
    "Cube2_1" IMeshData
      "transform" ITransform scale(1,1,1), translate(0,0,-2.5)
    "transform" ITransform scale(1,1,1), translate(0,0,-2.5)
```
With this change, the scene graph looks like this (The transform component for the mesh is removed, and only the one from its parent bone is applied):
```
"ROOT" IBoneData
  "Cube1" IBoneData
    "transform" ITransform scale(1,1,1), translate(0,0,0)
    "Cube1_2" IMeshData
  "Cube2" IBoneData
    "transform" ITransform scale(1,1,1), translate(0,0,-2.5)
    "Cube2_2" IMeshData
```
